revision.comment:
Created page with "{{Infobox new religious movement | name = Yamakage Shinto | native_name = 山蔭神道 | classification = [[Shinto]] ([[Ko-Shintō]]) | founder = Yamakage Inbei (revival)
Nakayama Tadateru (organization) | leader = Hitoyoshi Yamakage | key_people = [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (79th Grand Master) | origin_date = Late [[Edo period]] (revival)
1954 (registered) | origin_place = [[Japan]] | headquarters = [[Hamamatsu]] (浜松市), [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} '''Yamakage Shi..."
revision.text:
{{Infobox new religious movement | name = Yamakage Shinto | native_name = 山蔭神道 | classification = [[Shinto]] ([[Ko-Shintō]]) | founder = Yamakage Inbei (revival)
Nakayama Tadateru (organization) | leader = Hitoyoshi Yamakage | key_people = [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (79th Grand Master) | origin_date = Late [[Edo period]] (revival)
1954 (registered) | origin_place = [[Japan]] | headquarters = [[Hamamatsu]] (浜松市), [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} '''Yamakage Shinto''' ({{lang-ja|山蔭神道}}, ''Yamakage Shintō'') is a sect of [[Shinto]], specifically classified under the banner of ''[[Ko-Shintō]]'' (Ancient Shinto / 古神道). It is a distinct religious movement that claims a lineage predating the organized [[State Shinto]] (国家神道) of the Meiji era, focusing on esoteric rituals, spiritual healing, and the direct experience of ''[[Kami]]'' (divine spirit/energy).{{cite web |title=Yamakage Shintō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126 |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2024-01-28}} The group gained international attention through the English-language writings of its 79th Grand Master, '''Motohisa Yamakage''' (山蔭基央, 1925–2013), particularly his book ''The Essence of Shinto''.{{cite book |last=Yamakage |first=Motohisa |title=The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart |publisher=Kodansha International |year=2006 |isbn=978-4770030443}} == History == According to the sect's tradition, Yamakage Shinto is transmitted by the Yamakage family, who claim to be an ancient Shinto lineage that served the [[Imperial House of Japan]]. The name "Yamakage" (山蔭) is traditionally interpreted by the sect to mean "Shelter of the Mountain," symbolizing the protection of the Emperor. While the family claims an ancient unwritten lineage, the organized religious structure emerged more recently. In the late [[Edo period]], ''Yamakage Inbei'' (山蔭員畢), identified as the 75th generation leader, revived "ancient traditions" and incorporated elements from [[Suika Shinto]] (垂加神道). Later, in 1890, the 77th leader ''Nakayama Tadateru'' (中山忠徳) organized the ''Kōdōkai'' (Imperial Way Society / 皇道会). The modern religious corporation, known formally as ''Tensha Yamakage Shinto Aishinkai'' (天社山蔭神道愛信会), was registered in 1954 under the leadership of ''Yamakage Motohisa'' (山蔭基央).{{cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Yamakage Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126}} == Beliefs and practices == Yamakage Shinto distinguishes itself from [[Jinja Shinto]] (Shrine Shinto / 神社神道) by emphasizing ''Ko-Shintō''—the "old" or "original" Shinto that supposedly existed before the introduction of Buddhism and the formalization of shrine worship. === Core tenets === * ''Universality'': Unlike nationalist interpretations of Shinto, Yamakage Shinto teaches that Shinto is a universal path accessible to all people, regardless of nationality. Motohisa Yamakage famously stated that Shinto is not a religion in the Western sense but a "natural law" or "path of nature." * ''Spirit of the Word (''Kotodama'')'': The sect places significant emphasis on ''[[Kotodama]]'' (言霊), the belief that mystical powers reside in words and names. * ''Kami'': Kami (神) are viewed not merely as mythological deities but as the fundamental energy or life force that permeates the universe. === Rituals === The sect practices specific esoteric rituals aimed at purification (''[[Harae]]'' / 祓) and spiritual pacification (''[[Chinkon]]'' / 鎮魂). * ''Jireishi'' (Self-Spirit Prayer / 自霊詞): A central ritual involving prayer for one's own spirit. * ''Jireihai'' (Self-Spirit Veneration / 自霊拝): A rite honoring the practitioner's own spirit, believed to originate from ancient imperial rituals involving the sacred mirror. * ''Okinagaho'' (Long-Breath Method / 息長法): A deep breathing and meditation technique used to purify the mind and body. == Leadership == The leadership is hereditary, passed down through the Yamakage family. * ''79th Grand Master'': [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (山蔭基央, 1925–2013). He was instrumental in introducing Yamakage Shinto to the West. He retired in 2005. * ''80th Grand Master'': ''Hitoyoshi Yamakage'' (山蔭仁善). == Notable works == * ''The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart'' (2006) – Written by Motohisa Yamakage, this book is one of the few detailed English-language resources on the sect's philosophy. It covers topics such as purification, the nature of the soul, and the relevance of Shinto in the modern world. == See also == * [[Ko-Shintō]] (古神道) * [[Sect Shinto]] (教派神道) * [[Misogi]] (禊) * [[Aikido]] (historically connected to Omoto-kyo and Ko-Shinto philosophies) == References == {{reflist}} == External links == * [https://www.yamakageshinto.jp/ Official Website of Yamakage Shinto] (Japanese) [[Category:Shinto]]

{{Infobox new religious movement | name = Yamakage Shinto | native_name = 山蔭神道 | classification = [[Shinto]] ([[Ko-Shintō]]) | founder = Yamakage Inbei (revival)
Nakayama Tadateru (organization) | leader = Hitoyoshi Yamakage | key_people = [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (79th Grand Master) | origin_date = Late [[Edo period]] (revival)
1954 (registered) | origin_place = [[Japan]] | headquarters = [[Hamamatsu]] (浜松市), [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} '''Yamakage Shinto''' ({{lang-ja|山蔭神道}}, ''Yamakage Shintō'') is a sect of [[Shinto]], specifically classified under the banner of ''[[Ko-Shintō]]'' (Ancient Shinto / 古神道). It is a distinct religious movement that claims a lineage predating the organized [[State Shinto]] (国家神道) of the Meiji era, focusing on esoteric rituals, spiritual healing, and the direct experience of ''[[Kami]]'' (divine spirit/energy).{{cite web |title=Yamakage Shintō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126 |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2024-01-28}} The group gained international attention through the English-language writings of its 79th Grand Master, '''Motohisa Yamakage''' (山蔭基央, 1925–2013), particularly his book ''The Essence of Shinto''.{{cite book |last=Yamakage |first=Motohisa |title=The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart |publisher=Kodansha International |year=2006 |isbn=978-4770030443}} == History == According to the sect's tradition, Yamakage Shinto is transmitted by the Yamakage family, who claim to be an ancient Shinto lineage that served the [[Imperial House of Japan]]. The name "Yamakage" (山蔭) is traditionally interpreted by the sect to mean "Shelter of the Mountain," symbolizing the protection of the Emperor. While the family claims an ancient unwritten lineage, the organized religious structure emerged more recently. In the late [[Edo period]], ''Yamakage Inbei'' (山蔭員畢), identified as the 75th generation leader, revived "ancient traditions" and incorporated elements from [[Suika Shinto]] (垂加神道). Later, in 1890, the 77th leader ''Nakayama Tadateru'' (中山忠徳) organized the ''Kōdōkai'' (Imperial Way Society / 皇道会). The modern religious corporation, known formally as ''Tensha Yamakage Shinto Aishinkai'' (天社山蔭神道愛信会), was registered in 1954 under the leadership of ''Yamakage Motohisa'' (山蔭基央).{{cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Yamakage Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126}} == Beliefs and practices == Yamakage Shinto distinguishes itself from [[Jinja Shinto]] (Shrine Shinto / 神社神道) by emphasizing ''Ko-Shintō''—the "old" or "original" Shinto that supposedly existed before the introduction of Buddhism and the formalization of shrine worship. === Core tenets === * ''Universality'': Unlike nationalist interpretations of Shinto, Yamakage Shinto teaches that Shinto is a universal path accessible to all people, regardless of nationality. Motohisa Yamakage famously stated that Shinto is not a religion in the Western sense but a "natural law" or "path of nature." * ''Spirit of the Word (''Kotodama'')'': The sect places significant emphasis on ''[[Kotodama]]'' (言霊), the belief that mystical powers reside in words and names. * ''Kami'': Kami (神) are viewed not merely as mythological deities but as the fundamental energy or life force that permeates the universe. === Rituals === The sect practices specific esoteric rituals aimed at purification (''[[Harae]]'' / 祓) and spiritual pacification (''[[Chinkon]]'' / 鎮魂). * ''Jireishi'' (Self-Spirit Prayer / 自霊詞): A central ritual involving prayer for one's own spirit. * ''Jireihai'' (Self-Spirit Veneration / 自霊拝): A rite honoring the practitioner's own spirit, believed to originate from ancient imperial rituals involving the sacred mirror. * ''Okinagaho'' (Long-Breath Method / 息長法): A deep breathing and meditation technique used to purify the mind and body. == Leadership == The leadership is hereditary, passed down through the Yamakage family. * ''79th Grand Master'': [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (山蔭基央, 1925–2013). He was instrumental in introducing Yamakage Shinto to the West. He retired in 2005. * ''80th Grand Master'': ''Hitoyoshi Yamakage'' (山蔭仁善). == Notable works == * ''The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart'' (2006) – Written by Motohisa Yamakage, this book is one of the few detailed English-language resources on the sect's philosophy. It covers topics such as purification, the nature of the soul, and the relevance of Shinto in the modern world. == See also == * [[Ko-Shintō]] (古神道) * [[Sect Shinto]] (教派神道) * [[Misogi]] (禊) * [[Aikido]] (historically connected to Omoto-kyo and Ko-Shinto philosophies) == References == {{reflist}} ''Note'': Generated by Google Gemini 3 Pro. Please check for errors. == External links == * [https://www.yamakageshinto.jp/ Official Website of Yamakage Shinto] (Japanese) [[Category:Shinto]]

{{Infobox new religious movement | name = Yamakage Shinto | native_name = 山蔭神道 | classification = [[Shinto]] ([[Ko-Shintō]]) | founder = Yamakage Inbei (revival)
Nakayama Tadateru (organization) | leader = Hitoyoshi Yamakage | key_people = [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (79th Grand Master) | origin_date = Late [[Edo period]] (revival)
1954 (registered) | origin_place = [[Japan]] | headquarters = [[Hamamatsu]] (浜松市), [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} '''Yamakage Shinto''' ({{lang-ja|山蔭神道}}, ''Yamakage Shintō'') is a sect of [[Shinto]], specifically classified under the banner of ''[[Ko-Shintō]]'' (Ancient Shinto / 古神道). It is a distinct religious movement that claims a lineage predating the organized [[State Shinto]] (国家神道) of the Meiji era, focusing on esoteric rituals, spiritual healing, and the direct experience of ''[[Kami]]'' (divine spirit/energy).{{cite web |title=Yamakage Shintō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126 |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2024-01-28}} The group gained international attention through the English-language writings of its 79th Grand Master, '''Motohisa Yamakage''' (山蔭基央, 1925–2013), particularly his book ''The Essence of Shinto''.{{cite book |last=Yamakage |first=Motohisa |title=The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart |publisher=Kodansha International |year=2006 |isbn=978-4770030443}} == History == According to the sect's tradition, Yamakage Shinto is transmitted by the Yamakage family, who claim to be an ancient Shinto lineage that served the [[Imperial House of Japan]]. The name "Yamakage" (山蔭) is traditionally interpreted by the sect to mean "Shelter of the Mountain," symbolizing the protection of the Emperor. While the family claims an ancient unwritten lineage, the organized religious structure emerged more recently. In the late [[Edo period]], ''Yamakage Inbei'' (山蔭員畢), identified as the 75th generation leader, revived "ancient traditions" and incorporated elements from [[Suika Shinto]] (垂加神道). Later, in 1890, the 77th leader ''Nakayama Tadateru'' (中山忠徳) organized the ''Kōdōkai'' (Imperial Way Society / 皇道会). The modern religious corporation, known formally as ''Tensha Yamakage Shinto Aishinkai'' (天社山蔭神道愛信会), was registered in 1954 under the leadership of ''Yamakage Motohisa'' (山蔭基央).{{cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Yamakage Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126}} == Beliefs and practices == Yamakage Shinto distinguishes itself from [[Jinja Shinto]] (Shrine Shinto / 神社神道) by emphasizing ''Ko-Shintō''—the "old" or "original" Shinto that supposedly existed before the introduction of Buddhism and the formalization of shrine worship. === Core tenets === * ''Universality'': Unlike nationalist interpretations of Shinto, Yamakage Shinto teaches that Shinto is a universal path accessible to all people, regardless of nationality. Motohisa Yamakage famously stated that Shinto is not a religion in the Western sense but a "natural law" or "path of nature." * ''Spirit of the Word (''Kotodama'')'': The sect places significant emphasis on ''[[Kotodama]]'' (言霊), the belief that mystical powers reside in words and names. * ''Kami'': Kami (神) are viewed not merely as mythological deities but as the fundamental energy or life force that permeates the universe. === Rituals === The sect practices specific esoteric rituals aimed at purification (''[[Harae]]'' / 祓) and spiritual pacification (''[[Chinkon]]'' / 鎮魂). * ''Jireishi'' (Self-Spirit Prayer / 自霊詞): A central ritual involving prayer for one's own spirit. * ''Jireihai'' (Self-Spirit Veneration / 自霊拝): A rite honoring the practitioner's own spirit, believed to originate from ancient imperial rituals involving the sacred mirror. * ''Okinagaho'' (Long-Breath Method / 息長法): A deep breathing and meditation technique used to purify the mind and body. == Leadership == The leadership is hereditary, passed down through the Yamakage family. * ''79th Grand Master'': [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (山蔭基央, 1925–2013). He was instrumental in introducing Yamakage Shinto to the West. He retired in 2005. * ''80th Grand Master'': ''Hitoyoshi Yamakage'' (山蔭仁善). == Notable works == * ''The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart'' (2006) – Written by Motohisa Yamakage, this book is one of the few detailed English-language resources on the sect's philosophy. It covers topics such as purification, the nature of the soul, and the relevance of Shinto in the modern world. == See also == * [[Ko-Shintō]] (古神道) * [[Sect Shinto]] (教派神道) * [[Misogi]] (禊) * [[Aikido]] (historically connected to Omoto-kyo and Ko-Shinto philosophies) == References == {{reflist}} ''Note'': Generated by Google Gemini 3 Pro. Please check for errors. == External links == * [https://www.yamakageshinto.jp/ Official Website of Yamakage Shinto] (Japanese) [[Category:Shinto]][[Category:orphaned pages]]

{{Infobox new religious movement | name = Yamakage Shinto | native_name = 山蔭神道 | classification = [[Shinto]] ([[Ko-Shintō]]) | founder = Yamakage Inbei (revival)
Nakayama Tadateru (organization) | leader = Hitoyoshi Yamakage | key_people = [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (79th Grand Master) | origin_date = Late [[Edo period]] (revival)
1954 (registered) | origin_place = [[Japan]] | headquarters = [[Hamamatsu]] (浜松市), [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} '''Yamakage Shinto''' ({{lang-ja|山蔭神道}}, ''Yamakage Shintō'') is a sect of [[Shinto]], specifically classified under the banner of ''[[Ko-Shintō]]'' (Ancient Shinto / 古神道). It is a distinct religious movement that claims a lineage predating the organized [[State Shinto]] (国家神道) of the Meiji era, focusing on esoteric rituals, spiritual healing, and the direct experience of ''[[Kami]]'' (divine spirit/energy).{{cite web |title=Yamakage Shintō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126 |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2024-01-28}} The group gained international attention through the English-language writings of its 79th Grand Master, '''Motohisa Yamakage''' (山蔭基央, 1925–2013), particularly his book ''The Essence of Shinto''.{{cite book |last=Yamakage |first=Motohisa |title=The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart |publisher=Kodansha International |year=2006 |isbn=978-4770030443}} == History == According to the sect's tradition, Yamakage Shinto is transmitted by the Yamakage family, who claim to be an ancient Shinto lineage that served the [[Imperial House of Japan]]. The name "Yamakage" (山蔭) is traditionally interpreted by the sect to mean "Shelter of the Mountain," symbolizing the protection of the Emperor. While the family claims an ancient unwritten lineage, the organized religious structure emerged more recently. In the late [[Edo period]], ''Yamakage Inbei'' (山蔭員畢), identified as the 75th generation leader, revived "ancient traditions" and incorporated elements from [[Suika Shinto]] (垂加神道). Later, in 1890, the 77th leader ''Nakayama Tadateru'' (中山忠徳) organized the ''Kōdōkai'' (Imperial Way Society / 皇道会). The modern religious corporation, known formally as ''Tensha Yamakage Shinto Aishinkai'' (天社山蔭神道愛信会), was registered in 1954 under the leadership of ''Yamakage Motohisa'' (山蔭基央).{{cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Yamakage Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126}} == Beliefs and practices == Yamakage Shinto distinguishes itself from [[Jinja Shinto]] (Shrine Shinto / 神社神道) by emphasizing ''Ko-Shintō''—the "old" or "original" Shinto that supposedly existed before the introduction of Buddhism and the formalization of shrine worship. === Core tenets === * ''Universality'': Unlike nationalist interpretations of Shinto, Yamakage Shinto teaches that Shinto is a universal path accessible to all people, regardless of nationality. Motohisa Yamakage famously stated that Shinto is not a religion in the Western sense but a "natural law" or "path of nature." * ''Spirit of the Word (''Kotodama'')'': The sect places significant emphasis on ''[[Kotodama]]'' (言霊), the belief that mystical powers reside in words and names. * ''Kami'': Kami (神) are viewed not merely as mythological deities but as the fundamental energy or life force that permeates the universe. === Rituals === The sect practices specific esoteric rituals aimed at purification (''[[Harae]]'' / 祓) and spiritual pacification (''[[Chinkon]]'' / 鎮魂). * ''Jireishi'' (Self-Spirit Prayer / 自霊詞): A central ritual involving prayer for one's own spirit. * ''Jireihai'' (Self-Spirit Veneration / 自霊拝): A rite honoring the practitioner's own spirit, believed to originate from ancient imperial rituals involving the sacred mirror. * ''Okinagaho'' (Long-Breath Method / 息長法): A deep breathing and meditation technique used to purify the mind and body. == Leadership == The leadership is hereditary, passed down through the Yamakage family. * ''79th Grand Master'': [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (山蔭基央, 1925–2013). He was instrumental in introducing Yamakage Shinto to the West. He retired in 2005. * ''80th Grand Master'': ''Hitoyoshi Yamakage'' (山蔭仁善). == Notable works == * ''The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart'' (2006) – Written by Motohisa Yamakage, this book is one of the few detailed English-language resources on the sect's philosophy. It covers topics such as purification, the nature of the soul, and the relevance of Shinto in the modern world. == See also == * [[Ko-Shintō]] (古神道) * [[Sect Shinto]] (教派神道) * [[Misogi]] (禊) * [[Aikido]] (historically connected to Omoto-kyo and Ko-Shinto philosophies) == References == {{reflist}} ''Note'': Generated by Google Gemini 3 Pro. Please check for errors. == External links == * [https://www.yamakageshinto.jp/ Official Website of Yamakage Shinto] (Japanese) [[Category:Shinto]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]]

{{Infobox new religious movement | name = Yamakage Shinto | native_name = 山蔭神道 | classification = [[Shinto]] ([[Ko-Shintō]]) | founder = Yamakage Inbei (revival)
Nakayama Tadateru (organization) | leader = Hitoyoshi Yamakage | key_people = [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (79th Grand Master) | origin_date = Late [[Edo period]] (revival)
1954 (registered) | origin_place = [[Japan]] | headquarters = [[Hamamatsu]] (浜松市), [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} '''Yamakage Shinto''' ({{lang-ja|山蔭神道}}, ''Yamakage Shintō'') is a sect of [[Shinto]], specifically classified under the banner of ''[[Ko-Shintō]]'' (Ancient Shinto / 古神道). It is a distinct religious movement that claims a lineage predating the organized [[State Shinto]] (国家神道) of the Meiji era, focusing on esoteric rituals, spiritual healing, and the direct experience of ''[[Kami]]'' (divine spirit/energy).{{cite web |title=Yamakage Shintō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126 |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2024-01-28}} The group gained international attention through the English-language writings of its 79th Grand Master, '''Motohisa Yamakage''' (山蔭基央, 1925–2013), particularly his book ''The Essence of Shinto''.{{cite book |last=Yamakage |first=Motohisa |title=The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart |publisher=Kodansha International |year=2006 |isbn=978-4770030443}} == History == According to the sect's tradition, Yamakage Shinto is transmitted by the Yamakage family, who claim to be an ancient Shinto lineage that served the [[Imperial House of Japan]]. The name "Yamakage" (山蔭) is traditionally interpreted by the sect to mean "Shelter of the Mountain," symbolizing the protection of the Emperor. While the family claims an ancient unwritten lineage, the organized religious structure emerged more recently. In the late [[Edo period]], ''Yamakage Inbei'' (山蔭員畢), identified as the 75th generation leader, revived "ancient traditions" and incorporated elements from [[Suika Shinto]] (垂加神道). Later, in 1890, the 77th leader ''Nakayama Tadateru'' (中山忠徳) organized the ''Kōdōkai'' (Imperial Way Society / 皇道会). The modern religious corporation, known formally as ''Tensha Yamakage Shinto Aishinkai'' (天社山蔭神道愛信会), was registered in 1954 under the leadership of ''Yamakage Motohisa'' (山蔭基央).{{cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Yamakage Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126}} == Beliefs and practices == Yamakage Shinto distinguishes itself from [[Jinja Shinto]] (Shrine Shinto / 神社神道) by emphasizing ''Ko-Shintō''—the "old" or "original" Shinto that supposedly existed before the introduction of Buddhism and the formalization of shrine worship. === Core tenets === * ''Universality'': Unlike nationalist interpretations of Shinto, Yamakage Shinto teaches that Shinto is a universal path accessible to all people, regardless of nationality. Motohisa Yamakage famously stated that Shinto is not a religion in the Western sense but a "natural law" or "path of nature." * ''Spirit of the Word (''Kotodama'')'': The sect places significant emphasis on ''[[Kotodama]]'' (言霊), the belief that mystical powers reside in words and names. * ''Kami'': Kami (神) are viewed not merely as mythological deities but as the fundamental energy or life force that permeates the universe. === Rituals === The sect practices specific esoteric rituals aimed at purification (''[[Harae]]'' / 祓) and spiritual pacification (''[[Chinkon]]'' / 鎮魂). * ''Jireishi'' (Self-Spirit Prayer / 自霊詞): A central ritual involving prayer for one's own spirit. * ''Jireihai'' (Self-Spirit Veneration / 自霊拝): A rite honoring the practitioner's own spirit, believed to originate from ancient imperial rituals involving the sacred mirror. * ''Okinagaho'' (Long-Breath Method / 息長法): A deep breathing and meditation technique used to purify the mind and body. == Leadership == The leadership is hereditary, passed down through the Yamakage family. * ''79th Grand Master'': [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (山蔭基央, 1925–2013). He was instrumental in introducing Yamakage Shinto to the West. He retired in 2005. * ''80th Grand Master'': ''Hitoyoshi Yamakage'' (山蔭仁善). == Notable works == * ''The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart'' (2006) – Written by Motohisa Yamakage, this book is one of the few detailed English-language resources on the sect's philosophy. It covers topics such as purification, the nature of the soul, and the relevance of Shinto in the modern world. == See also == * [[Ko-Shintō]] (古神道) * [[Sect Shinto]] (教派神道) * [[Misogi]] (禊) * [[Aikido]] (historically connected to Omoto-kyo and Ko-Shinto philosophies) == References == {{reflist}} ''Note'': Generated by Google Gemini 3 Pro. Please check for errors. == External links == * [https://www.yamakageshinto.jp/ Official Website of Yamakage Shinto] (Japanese) [[Category:Shinto]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]]

{{Infobox new religious movement | name = Yamakage Shinto | native_name = 山蔭神道 | classification = [[Shinto]] ([[Ko-Shintō]]) | founder = Yamakage Inbei (revival)
Nakayama Tadateru (organization) | leader = Hitoyoshi Yamakage | key_people = [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (79th Grand Master) | origin_date = Late [[Edo period]] (revival)
1954 (registered) | origin_place = [[Japan]] | headquarters = [[Hamamatsu]] (浜松市), [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} '''Yamakage Shinto''' ({{lang-ja|山蔭神道}}, ''Yamakage Shintō'') is a sect of [[Shinto]], specifically classified under the banner of ''[[Ko-Shintō]]'' (Ancient Shinto / 古神道). It is a distinct religious movement that claims a lineage predating the organized [[State Shinto]] (国家神道) of the Meiji era, focusing on esoteric rituals, spiritual healing, and the direct experience of ''[[Kami]]'' (divine spirit/energy).{{cite web |title=Yamakage Shintō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126 |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2024-01-28}} The group gained international attention through the English-language writings of its 79th Grand Master, '''Motohisa Yamakage''' (山蔭基央, 1925–2013), particularly his book ''The Essence of Shinto''.{{cite book |last=Yamakage |first=Motohisa |title=The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart |publisher=Kodansha International |year=2006 |isbn=978-4770030443}} == History == According to the sect's tradition, Yamakage Shinto is transmitted by the Yamakage family, who claim to be an ancient Shinto lineage that served the [[Imperial House of Japan]]. The name "Yamakage" (山蔭) is traditionally interpreted by the sect to mean "Shelter of the Mountain," symbolizing the protection of the Emperor. While the family claims an ancient unwritten lineage, the organized religious structure emerged more recently. In the late [[Edo period]], ''Yamakage Inbei'' (山蔭員畢), identified as the 75th generation leader, revived "ancient traditions" and incorporated elements from [[Suika Shinto]] (垂加神道). Later, in 1890, the 77th leader ''Nakayama Tadateru'' (中山忠徳) organized the ''Kōdōkai'' (Imperial Way Society / 皇道会). The modern religious corporation, known formally as ''Tensha Yamakage Shinto Aishinkai'' (天社山蔭神道愛信会), was registered in 1954 under the leadership of ''Yamakage Motohisa'' (山蔭基央).{{cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Yamakage Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126}} == Beliefs and practices == Yamakage Shinto distinguishes itself from [[Jinja Shinto]] (Shrine Shinto / 神社神道) by emphasizing ''Ko-Shintō''—the "old" or "original" Shinto that supposedly existed before the introduction of Buddhism and the formalization of shrine worship. === Core tenets === * ''Universality'': Unlike nationalist interpretations of Shinto, Yamakage Shinto teaches that Shinto is a universal path accessible to all people, regardless of nationality. Motohisa Yamakage famously stated that Shinto is not a religion in the Western sense but a "natural law" or "path of nature." * ''Spirit of the Word (''Kotodama'')'': The sect places significant emphasis on ''[[Kotodama]]'' (言霊), the belief that mystical powers reside in words and names. * ''Kami'': Kami (神) are viewed not merely as mythological deities but as the fundamental energy or life force that permeates the universe. === Rituals === The sect practices specific esoteric rituals aimed at purification (''[[Harae]]'' / 祓) and spiritual pacification (''[[Chinkon]]'' / 鎮魂). * ''Jireishi'' (Self-Spirit Prayer / 自霊詞): A central ritual involving prayer for one's own spirit. * ''Jireihai'' (Self-Spirit Veneration / 自霊拝): A rite honoring the practitioner's own spirit, believed to originate from ancient imperial rituals involving the sacred mirror. * ''Okinagaho'' (Long-Breath Method / 息長法): A deep breathing and meditation technique used to purify the mind and body. == Leadership == The leadership is hereditary, passed down through the Yamakage family. * ''79th Grand Master'': [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (山蔭基央, 1925–2013). He was instrumental in introducing Yamakage Shinto to the West. He retired in 2005. * ''80th Grand Master'': ''Hitoyoshi Yamakage'' (山蔭仁善). == Notable works == * ''The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart'' (2006) – Written by Motohisa Yamakage, this book is one of the few detailed English-language resources on the sect's philosophy. It covers topics such as purification, the nature of the soul, and the relevance of Shinto in the modern world. == See also == * [[Ko-Shintō]] (古神道) * [[Sect Shinto]] (教派神道) * [[Misogi]] (禊) * [[Aikido]] (historically connected to Omoto-kyo and Ko-Shinto philosophies) == References == {{reflist}} ''Note'': Generated by Google Gemini 3 Pro. Please check for errors. == External links == * [https://www.yamakageshinto.jp/ Official Website of Yamakage Shinto] (Japanese) [[Category:Shinto]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]][[Category:orphaned pages]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Orphaned pages]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23455888809|manual triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
{{Infobox new religious movement | name = Yamakage Shinto | native_name = 山蔭神道 | classification = [[Shinto]] ([[Ko-Shintō]]) | founder = Yamakage Inbei (revival)
Nakayama Tadateru (organization) | leader = Hitoyoshi Yamakage | key_people = [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (79th Grand Master) | origin_date = Late [[Edo period]] (revival)
1954 (registered) | origin_place = [[Japan]] | headquarters = [[Hamamatsu]] (浜松市), [[Shizuoka Prefecture]] }} '''Yamakage Shinto''' ({{lang-ja|山蔭神道}}, ''Yamakage Shintō'') is a sect of [[Shinto]], specifically classified under the banner of ''[[Ko-Shintō]]'' (Ancient Shinto / 古神道). It is a distinct religious movement that claims a lineage predating the organized [[State Shinto]] (国家神道) of the Meiji era, focusing on esoteric rituals, spiritual healing, and the direct experience of ''[[Kami]]'' (divine spirit/energy).{{cite web |title=Yamakage Shintō |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126 |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=2024-01-28}} The group gained international attention through the English-language writings of its 79th Grand Master, '''Motohisa Yamakage''' (山蔭基央, 1925–2013), particularly his book ''The Essence of Shinto''.{{cite book |last=Yamakage |first=Motohisa |title=The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart |publisher=Kodansha International |year=2006 |isbn=978-4770030443}} == History == According to the sect's tradition, Yamakage Shinto is transmitted by the Yamakage family, who claim to be an ancient Shinto lineage that served the [[Imperial House of Japan]]. The name "Yamakage" (山蔭) is traditionally interpreted by the sect to mean "Shelter of the Mountain," symbolizing the protection of the Emperor. While the family claims an ancient unwritten lineage, the organized religious structure emerged more recently. In the late [[Edo period]], ''Yamakage Inbei'' (山蔭員畢), identified as the 75th generation leader, revived "ancient traditions" and incorporated elements from [[Suika Shinto]] (垂加神道). Later, in 1890, the 77th leader ''Nakayama Tadateru'' (中山忠徳) organized the ''Kōdōkai'' (Imperial Way Society / 皇道会). The modern religious corporation, known formally as ''Tensha Yamakage Shinto Aishinkai'' (天社山蔭神道愛信会), was registered in 1954 under the leadership of ''Yamakage Motohisa'' (山蔭基央).{{cite web |title=Encyclopedia of Shinto: Yamakage Shinto |publisher=Kokugakuin University |url=https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=9126}} == Beliefs and practices == Yamakage Shinto distinguishes itself from [[Jinja Shinto]] (Shrine Shinto / 神社神道) by emphasizing ''Ko-Shintō''—the "old" or "original" Shinto that supposedly existed before the introduction of Buddhism and the formalization of shrine worship. === Core tenets === * ''Universality'': Unlike nationalist interpretations of Shinto, Yamakage Shinto teaches that Shinto is a universal path accessible to all people, regardless of nationality. Motohisa Yamakage famously stated that Shinto is not a religion in the Western sense but a "natural law" or "path of nature." * ''Spirit of the Word (''Kotodama'')'': The sect places significant emphasis on ''[[Kotodama]]'' (言霊), the belief that mystical powers reside in words and names. * ''Kami'': Kami (神) are viewed not merely as mythological deities but as the fundamental energy or life force that permeates the universe. === Rituals === The sect practices specific esoteric rituals aimed at purification (''[[Harae]]'' / 祓) and spiritual pacification (''[[Chinkon]]'' / 鎮魂). * ''Jireishi'' (Self-Spirit Prayer / 自霊詞): A central ritual involving prayer for one's own spirit. * ''Jireihai'' (Self-Spirit Veneration / 自霊拝): A rite honoring the practitioner's own spirit, believed to originate from ancient imperial rituals involving the sacred mirror. * ''Okinagaho'' (Long-Breath Method / 息長法): A deep breathing and meditation technique used to purify the mind and body. == Leadership == The leadership is hereditary, passed down through the Yamakage family. * ''79th Grand Master'': [[Motohisa Yamakage]] (山蔭基央, 1925–2013). He was instrumental in introducing Yamakage Shinto to the West. He retired in 2005. * ''80th Grand Master'': ''Hitoyoshi Yamakage'' (山蔭仁善). == Notable works == * ''The Essence of Shinto: Japan's Spiritual Heart'' (2006) – Written by Motohisa Yamakage, this book is one of the few detailed English-language resources on the sect's philosophy. It covers topics such as purification, the nature of the soul, and the relevance of Shinto in the modern world. == See also == * [[Ko-Shintō]] (古神道) * [[Sect Shinto]] (教派神道) * [[Misogi]] (禊) * [[Aikido]] (historically connected to Omoto-kyo and Ko-Shinto philosophies) == References == {{reflist}} ''Note'': Generated by Google Gemini 3 Pro. Please check for errors. == External links == * [https://www.yamakageshinto.jp/ Official Website of Yamakage Shinto] (Japanese) [[Category:Shinto]]
